name: Monstera lechleriana 'Albo Variegated'
origin: Central America to northwestern South America; variegated collector form in cultivation.
aboutText: Monstera lechleriana 'Albo Variegated' is an exceptional climbing hemiepiphyte native from Central America to northwestern South America. Distinct from the standard deliciosa, it features elongated ovate to elliptic leaves with a strong midrib and fewer, larger, centrally placed fenestrations. The albo variegation pattern displays highly striking sectors and marbling of white to cream across the green leaf surface. It climbs efficiently on thick stems, anchored by robust aerial roots, and prefers warm, humid tropical conditions with bright indirect light to support mature fenestrated growth.

quickFacts.light: Bright, indirect filter - higher light for albo contrast

morphology.leafShape: Elongated ovate to elliptic with central fenestrations
morphology.variegation: Albo (white to cream) sectors and marbling
morphology.growthHabit: Climbing with thick stems and aerial roots

propagation.notes: Variegation carries reliably through cuttings. Select cuttings with strong variegation expression — avoid predominantly green sections.

careGuide.substrate: Chunky, well-draining mix: 40% potting compost, 30% perlite, 20% orchid bark, 10% worm castings. Monsteras are more tolerant of moisture than most aroids but still need drainage.
careGuide.watering: Water when the top 3–5 cm is dry. These are forgiving plants but still susceptible to root rot if kept too wet. Water less in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70% preferred. More tolerant of typical UK indoor humidity than other aroids, though 60%+ encourages larger leaf development.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced fertiliser at half strength every 2 weeks from spring through summer. Monthly in winter or none at all.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months when roots emerge from drainage holes. Provide a moss pole or support — climbing encourages larger leaf production.

commonProblems.cause: Plant is juvenile, or insufficient light
commonProblems.fix: Ensure bright indirect light and allow the plant to mature — fenestrations develop with age and better conditions
Check soil moisture before watering; begin a regular fertilising routine in growing season

commonProblems.cause: Sunburn or cold water on leaves
commonProblems.fix: Avoid direct sun; water at the base and ensure water is room temperature

items: Verify variegation extends into the newest leaf — a fully green newest leaf on an 'albo' or 'mint' monstera is a sign of reversion
Check that at least one healthy node is present below the most recent leaf — this is what produces all future growth
Inspect petioles closely for scale insects (small brown bumps) which are common on monsteras

Compare the variegation level shown to recently sold comps — heavily variegated sections command a significant premium and sellers sometimes photograph the most variegated area rather than the whole plant
